a: Equipment-efficiency gains are not labor substitution unless they remove manufacturer hours or avoid hiring.

rho: AI can assist safety-critical engineering but cannot replace required accountable sign-off.

e: Component and service companies are outside this exact industry lens even if economically adjacent.

s5: Family-business succession evidence may be less representative here than in ordinary industrial manufacturing.

d5: EIA electricity and capacity growth do not translate one-for-one to domestic turbine manufacturing.
d5: GE Vernova is a dominant large OEM whose backlog may not be addressable by LMM firms.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

identity.title: Turbine and Turbine Generator Set Units Manufacturing

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Long-cycle engineered equipment and generator-set projects supplemented by installed-base replacement parts, planned outages, controls upgrades, long-term service agreements, and technical support; sales are negotiated directly or through major project channels.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal units, shells, aircraft turbines, stand-alone generators, wind-blade or tower manufacturers, turbine-component job shops, and independent repair providers classified outside NAICS 333611.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers repeatedly supplying complete nonaircraft steam, hydraulic, gas, and wind turbines or turbine-generator set units, together with manufacturer-provided parts, upgrades, technical support, and service, to external power, industrial, and infrastructure customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader 333600 engine, turbine, and power-transmission industry, where production occupations represented 47.86% of May 2023 employment and assemblers/fabricators 18.94%. NIST documents manufacturing AI uses in inspection, maintenance, forecasting, and process decisions. I map high exposure to proposals, configuration, documentation, schedules, procurement, service knowledge, and software; partial exposure to mechanical, electrical, controls, applications, and quality engineering; and low exposure to machining, welding, winding, assembly, balancing, test execution, installation, and field repair. The broad occupation mix may understate engineering in turbine OEMs and overstate it in standardized power-transmission products.
caveats: BLS data cover NAICS 333600 rather than six-digit 333611.
Large-OEM engineering and service mixes may not represent niche LMM builders.
Equipment-efficiency gains are not labor substitution unless they remove manufacturer hours or avoid hiring.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory across every identifiable independent LMM turbine builder, with time studies for proposals, engineering, assembly, testing, commissioning, parts, and service, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Turbines require extensive engineering, project, documentation, analytics, and service work, but precision fabrication, assembly, validation, and field execution remain physical and safety-critical.

d5.bridge: EIA projects US electricity-demand growth averaging 0.9%-1.6% annually through 2050 across cases and 50%-90% more installed generating capacity by 2050. GE Vernova reports 2025 Power equipment and service remaining performance obligations of $94.4 billion, 29% above 2024, driven mainly by gas-turbine equipment and contractual services, while its wind-turbine unit orders fell from 1,212 in 2024 to 854 in 2025. I use electricity growth and large-OEM backlog as demand proxies, then temper them to constant-price five-year demand for the current US LMM turbine equipment and service basket. Large-OEM backlog and data-center-driven gas demand may overstate accessible LMM growth; wind weakness and long project cycles add downside.
caveats: EIA electricity and capacity growth do not translate one-for-one to domestic turbine manufacturing.
GE Vernova is a dominant large OEM whose backlog may not be addressable by LMM firms.
Wind and gas order trends diverge, while steam and hydro are project-specific.
d5.change_evidence: Resolved LMM builder backlog and book-to-bill, US generator interconnection and construction data by turbine technology, installed-base outage schedules, repowering volumes, and unit orders would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Rising electricity load, data centers, grid reliability, gas capacity, hydro refurbishment, wind repowering, and installed-base service support demand. Permitting, interconnection, policy shifts, OEM concentration, supply constraints, and divergent gas, wind, steam, and hydro cycles widen the range.

caveats: The provided firm count is margin-bridged and especially vulnerable to establishment-versus-firm and product-classification error in an industry dominated by large OEM groups.
Component and service companies are outside this exact industry lens even if economically adjacent.
e.change_evidence: Resolve all 38 estimated in-band firms to ultimate ownership and actual products, then verify complete-unit manufacturing, installed base, service revenue, certifications, customer concentration, normalized EBITDA, and warranty obligations through commercial data and direct calls.
e.rationale: True independent LMM manufacturers of complete turbines or turbine-generator sets should be transferable where they have certified designs, an installed base, service capability, and repeat external customers. The provided count may include small establishments of large groups, captive units, component suppliers, or firms classified by legacy activity; certification, warranty, working capital, owner engineering dependence, and customer concentration reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 333611 ($2,441,762,625) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 333611 ($11,982,937,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $427,032 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 333611); class <5: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,467,909 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $288,004 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 24 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,824,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $358,000 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,450,562 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $873,200 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 38 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,258,211 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,601,261 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $67,124,800 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,169,886 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $321,645,750 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $63,106,896 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 38

caveats: The operator may be a large global OEM rather than an LMM supplier.
Technology substitution can remove turbine units even as total electricity demand rises.
o.change_evidence: Utility and industrial-owner engineering interviews plus capacity-addition, repowering, life-extension, make-versus-buy, and non-turbine substitution data would quantify elimination and self-service of the current basket.
o.rationale: Generation still requires accountable manufacturers to design, fabricate, assemble, balance, test, certify, commission, and support physical turbines and generator sets. Software improves controls, diagnostics, and dispatch but cannot supply rotating machinery; limited loss can arise from non-turbine solar-plus-storage, customer self-maintenance, life extension, remanufacturing, and technology substitution.

caveats: GE Vernova's large service backlog demonstrates the model but not LMM retention rates.
Capture should be higher in proprietary installed-base parts and urgent support than in competitively tendered equipment.
q.change_evidence: Equipment and long-term service agreements plus five-year deal-level data for net price, productivity sharing, availability guarantees, penalties, warranty, parts, outages, and gross margin would reveal retention.
q.rationale: Installed-base compatibility, certified designs, proprietary controls, parts, outage response, performance history, and high switching or failure cost protect savings, especially in service. Utility and industrial buyers remain sophisticated, using competitive tenders, performance guarantees, liquidated damages, availability commitments, regulatory cost scrutiny, and long-term contract negotiation to share benefits.

rho.bridge: NIST reports 46% of manufacturers using AI tools and more than 80% expecting increased use within two years, while listing data, cost, skills, cyber, and legacy integration barriers. GE Vernova's filings show large equipment and contractual-service backlogs, illustrating the digital and service-rich workflow of turbine OEMs. I translate this broad evidence into the five-year implemented share of exposed work. Large-OEM digital maturity likely overstates LMM capability, while safety certification and utility cybersecurity slow deployment across both.
caveats: GE Vernova is a very large OEM and not representative of LMM implementation resources.
AI can assist safety-critical engineering but cannot replace required accountable sign-off.
rho.change_evidence: Comparable-builder deployment records showing baseline engineering and service hours, validation and certification results, cyber approval, false alarms, customer acceptance, and sustained labor savings would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: Proposal, engineering-assistant, scheduling, document, inspection, remote-diagnostic, and service-knowledge applications are feasible, but certification, deterministic controls, export and cybersecurity rules, long validation cycles, and field variability prevent full implementation.

s5.bridge: Deloitte surveyed 500 US family-enterprise members and found only 24% of current-generation respondents strongly agreed their succession plan could withstand departure of an important family employee. I use this as broad succession-pressure evidence and reduce potential exits for internal transitions, non-control investments, closures, and failed sales. The survey is not turbine-specific or a timing measure; the eligible population is likely unusually strategic and institutionally owned, which can reduce family-succession relevance while increasing corporate M&A.
caveats: No six-digit qualifying-transfer denominator was found.
Family-business succession evidence may be less representative here than in ordinary industrial manufacturing.
s5.change_evidence: Ultimate-owner and owner-age verification for the resolved eligible list plus ten years of closed control deals from PitchBook, GF Data, power-sector bankers, and turbine-industry records would narrow the range.
s5.rationale: Independent niche turbine builders may face succession or strategic acquisition, but the small eligible population, customer qualification, warranty tail, government review, and technical key-person risk can delay or prevent qualifying transfers.

principal_driver: Growing power demand and proprietary installed-base service create durable, operator-required work.
principal_weakness: The true transferable LMM population is uncertain in a capital-intensive industry dominated by large OEMs.
